source_metadata.title_raw: Dennis J. Stryker - A-18-179 - October 3, 2018 - San Diego

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 3, 2018

Dennis J. Stryker
Stryker Slev Law Group
3555 Fifth Avenue, Suite 205
San Diego, CA 92103

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-179

Dear Mr. Stryker:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of
Government Code section 1090. Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete
and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you
should contact us for additional advice.

Regarding our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Diego County
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a
written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that,
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

Does Section 1090 prohibit the City of San Diego from contracting with Rick Engineering
Company for the design of a project known as the Design of El Camino Real Road/Bridge
Widening (Project) where it previously provided information and data associated with the
development of environmental reports in connection with the Project?

CONCLUSION

No. Section 1090 does not prohibit the City from entering a contract for the Project's design
services with Rick Engineering because it was a subcontractor that provided technical analysis and
not subject to Section 1090. Rick Engineering did not impose considerable influence over the City
regarding the new contract, derived no unfair advantage in procuring the new contract by the work
it performed under the first contract, and did not participate in the making of a new contract through
its performance of the first contract.

File No. A-18-179
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTOR

You are an attorney seeking advice on behalf of Rick Engineering regarding the conflict of
interest provisions under Section 1090. We take verbatim the following relevant facts from your
letter:1

For a number of years the City has been in the process of
upgrading, realigning and replacing the bridge by which El Camino Real
crosses the San Dieguito River. The full Project includes the design of
the realignment of El Camino Real, the new intersection of El Camino
Real and Via de la Valle, and the bridge crossing over the San Dieguito
River. It was determined that the appropriate environmental documents
to be produced were an Environmental Impact Report (EIR) under the
California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA), and an Environmental
Impact Study (EIS) under the National Environmental Policy Act
(NEPA).

Federal funds make up approximately 80% of the funding for the
Project. Therefore, in addition to preparing environmental documents
that comply with both state and federal statutes, the City is required to
obtain review and input from a number of other agencies and
stakeholders.

The City began the environmental process for the Project
utilizing Earth Tech Engineering, but ultimately hired Hon Consulting to
continue assisting the City with the environmental process. Around
2004, Rick Engineering Company was asked by the City to compile and
supply data and studies to Hon Consulting for Hon's use and preparation
of the EIR/EIS. CEQA and NEPA guidelines require the development of
significant studies as well as a final report associated with those studies.
Supporting reports from varying types of professional organizations,
such as Rick Engineering Company, are required by the environmental
consultant ultimately preparing the EIR/EIS.

The type of information that Rick Engineering Company
provided to Hon Consulting consisted of:

1. Updating the Hydrology and Hydraulic studies associated with
the San Dieguito River as well as various tributaries;
2. Studies and calculations relating to the quantity of water
associated with the San Dieguito River and its tributary drainage areas;

1 The facts are taken from a letter by Rick Engineering Principal Les Hopper, which you submitted with your
request.

File No. A-18-179
Page No. 3

3. Creation of various exhibits based upon the studies prepared
by Rick Engineering Company for use and inclusion in the EIR/EIS;

4. Various studies to allow for the determination by the City of
the size and type of crossing (bridge type or culvert type per Federal
Highway Administration (FHWA) regulations) that would be necessary
at the San Dieguito River;

5. Review of impacts in the area from the proposed project.

In 2006, the first draft of the EIR was distributed by the City for
public review and comment. Based upon the comments received from
the public review, it was decided by City staff not to submit the EIR to
the City Council for approval and to continue with additional studies.

Rick Engineering Company was then asked to provide assistance
with those technical studies as directed by the City and/or Hon
Consulting in response to the public comments and review comments
from the stakeholder governmental agencies. The studies included
additional hydraulics and hydrology associated with the San Dieguito
River and the additional alternate alignments for El Camino Real, as well
as the bridge proposals and the intersection of El Camino Real and Vía
de la Valle.

Each of the draft alternative proposals and associated studies
referenced above originated through the public comment process,
stakeholder review and comment, or by the City's internal staff reviews.

In 2012, the EIS was officially separated from the EIR and RBF
Consulting was hired by the City to complete the EIS in accordance with
the requirements of NEPA. Rick Engineering Company provided its
prior studies and data, along with technical updates to those studies, to
RBF Consulting for inclusion in the EIS.

In 2016, the Final EIR was presented to the City, and it was
subsequently approved in 2017 by the City Council along with the Site
Development Permit (SDP) for the Project. The EIS is in final process
for approval by Caltrans and the City.

During the process of development, circulation, publication,
recirculation and final approval by the City of San Diego and Caltrans of
the EIR and the EIS, Rick Engineering Company did not provide
opinions or influence final decisions as to any of the alternative
alignments, bridge design or intersection configuration. All of the
alternatives were created through the City staff's review of comments
from the public, comments from other agencies/stakeholders, and review
of the data and supplied studies associated with the environmental
documents for the project.

File No. A-18-179
Page No. 4

ANALYSIS

A. Independent Contractors under Section 1090.

Interpreting the term "employees" as used in Section 1090, the California Supreme Court recently affirmed the long-standing rule from case law that independent contractors are not categorically excluded from Section 1090: "Liability under the statute extend to independent contractors who have duties to engage in or advise on public contracting." (People v. Superior Court (Sahlolbei) (2017) 3 Cal.5th 230, 233.) For example, an independent contractor for a state or local government agency that "has a hand in designing and developing the plans and specifications for the project, has made or participated in the making of a contract for the construction of the project and is therefore prohibited from entering a contract to complete the project. (Davis v. Fresno Unified School District (2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 261, 300-301.)

However, not all independent contractors are covered by Section 1090. (Sahlolbei, supra, at p. 240.) "An individual's status as an official under [Section 1090] turns on the extent to which the person influences an agency's contracting decisions or otherwise acts in a capacity that demands the public trust." (Ibid, quoting Hub City Solid Waste Services, Inc. v. City of Compton (Hub City) (2010) 186 Cal. App.4th 114, 125.) In Hub City, the court held that an independent contractor that exerts "considerable influence over the contracting decisions of a public agency" is subject to Section 1090. (Hub City, supra, at pp. 1124-1125.)

Previously we have advised that consultants who play a limited technical role and are removed from influencing the contracting decisions of a public agency may not be subject to Section 1090. (La Salle Advice Letter, No. A-17-074 and Green Advice Letter, No. A-16-084.) Likewise, in the Chadwick Advice Letter, No. A-15-147, we determined that only the primary consultant and a "highly involved sub-consultant" were subject to Section 1090. The subconsultants who provided technical input, reports, and similar information in a support role to the primary consultants, were not subject to Section 1090.

Generally, an independent contractor is prohibited from a subsequent project to complete a project only if the contractor has had extensive involvement in the initial design and development of the project and the nature of the contractor's involvement gives the contractor an unfair advantage in the subsequent award of the contract.

B. Rick Engineering is Not Subject to Section 1090.

The facts provided indicate that Rick Engineering mainly performed environmental studies related to technical aspects of the Project such as updates to the existing hydrology and hydraulic data associated with the San Dieguito River and corresponding tributaries and potential impacts to the area from the Project. The information and data compiled by Rick Engineering was then provided to other City consultants to assist them in the preparation of the EIR and EIS, which primarily determine whether or not the project will comply with federal and state environmental requirements.

File No. A-18-179
Page No. 5

The facts you provide suggest Rick Engineering had no responsibility, or any ability, to influence the City regarding the Project since it was simply responding to requests for data based upon input from public comments and stakeholder requests. As mentioned, Rick Engineering did not provide opinions or influence final decisions as to any of the alternative alignments, bridge design or intersection configuration. These decisions by the City were informed by its review of comments from the public, comments from other agencies/stakeholders, and review of the data and supplied studies associated with the environmental documents for the project.

Although Rick Engineering supplied information and data related to the environmental aspects of the Project, it was not in a position to exert considerable influence over the contracting decisions of the City with respect to the Project's design such that it gained an unfair advantage in the award of the Project's design services contract.

Accordingly, Rick Engineering is not subject to Section 1090, and the City may therefore enter into a contract with the company for the Project's design services.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

David Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Jack Woodside
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

JW:jgl

A.

Interpreting the term "employees" as used in Section 1090, the California Supreme Court
